chunk_summary: Guidelines for appetite test execution and admission triggers for SAM management, including outpatient eligibility, inpatient stabilization criteria, and specific protocols for infants under 6 months.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of the appetite test in determining outpatient eligibility for SAM management.
Learn the specific execution steps and criteria for passing or failing the appetite test.
Identify admission triggers for infants under 6 months based on feeding effectiveness and clinical signs.
Recognize anthropometric and practical considerations for SAM management in Indian operational pathways.
key_facts: The appetite test uses RUTF or locally prepared therapeutic feeds if RUTF is unavailable, with documented intake amounts.
Minimum intake thresholds for passing the appetite test: ≥15 g for <4 kg, ≥25 g for 4–7 kg, and ≥33 g for 7–10 kg.
Failure criteria include inability to meet intake thresholds, repeated vomiting, or lethargy during the test.
Infants under 6 months are admitted based on feeding effectiveness, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, serious bacterial infection, or bilateral pedal oedema.
Indian pathways use weight-for-length <-3 SD for infants >45 cm and visible severe wasting for very short infants.
reference_quote: Use the appetite test as an objective gate for outpatient eligibility and as an early marker of metabolic stability. (Source: National Health Mission, Participant manual for facility-based care of severe acute malnutrition)
document_key_topics: Appetite test execution for SAM management
Admission criteria for infants under 6 months
